text:
I am going to to do a sand battery to store my excess solar energy for days. 
My house sits on a sand dune like most in New Mexico (USA).
Heat loss to the ground would require insulation around the smaller sand bin , sized for my house. 
I have found little info on how long   a small sand bin holds heat and the insulation needed at the temperatures required.  500 deg c  is way too much for most insuations.  
I found recycled foam glass beads which would tolerate very high temps. Has anyone tried these?
I could imagine a trench filled with these surrounding the existing sand serving as the tank. More foam beads will be poured on top of the sand.  
Can anyone help out with the thermal calculations?

author: @graysonsmith7031
text: It seems like the most practical use for this is an oven. There isn't really any loss in efficiency over a heat pump or battery storage since the only way to heat up an oven is either gas or resistance elements because of the high temperature. Using a fresnel lens to heat up the sand rather than a solar panel and resistance element would mean you'd be capturing and storing solar energy as heat at close to 100% efficiency. Then the oven  it will be warm or ready to warm your oven whenever you need it. You could also slap a TEC to the sand and another to the back of the oven, so electricity is generated as the oven cools down and generates electricity from the sand if there is ever an excess of heat. So you could in theory have an oven that gets all its energy from sunlight and would generate electricity as the oven cools down or there is an abundance of solar thermal energy. With all except the electricity generation operating at near 100% efficiency. Even the TECs could operate at their best efficiency due ro rhe high heat and the heat passing from the oven/sand through thr TEC could even be used to heat water for residential use.

replies.author: @burgersnchips
replies.text: Ground source heat pumps are for that. You use the heat pump to remove the summer heat and send it into the ground and then reverse it in winter. If you don't use a heat pump and just send air through pipes, you're going to store only a day or two of heat before it balances off because the temperature differential is too small.

author: @wesbaumguardner8829
text: While this may be good for extending the heat release duration, the sand is also acting as an insulator, so you may not get the same total amount of heat if you are using this inside your home or shop to heat it.  in other words, you might be losing more heat through your exhaust.  Water might actually be a better option if you wrap copper plumbing tube around the stack and then route both ends to a tank so that the cold water from the bottom of the tank goes into the tube and, as the water heats up, it rises and feeds directly back into the top of the water tank while drawing cooler water from the bottom of the tank.  Through this method of cycling, it should bring evaporation down to a minimum, especially if you have a large tank.

author: @CairnOfDunnCroftPermaculture
text:
Anyone building something like this for long term use should be aware of one issue. As a rocket stove, steel is fine because it radiates so much heat it keeps the temperatures down. Same with a water jacket. But once you start surrounding the rocket core with insulative materials like clay and sand it can bring the temperatures in the core up significantly. Over time, the steel spalls and fails. This is why refractory materials are typically used in rocket mass heater systems. 
I'm a huge advocate of mass heating, my whole home is heated with one. It would probably be a good idea to consider installing a rocket mass heater rather than try and retrofit something like this to a conventional wood burner.

author: @yellowbird5411
text: Back in the day, a couple of centuries ago, rocks were heated by the fire and were put into metal buckets or containers and used to heat certain areas, like under the seats of carriages, in bedrooms, or wherever they were needed. Today, rocks are used for heating tents in camping, and they last a long time. I had started wondering about sand, as I live in Florida, and we don't really have rocks by the beach, just limestone, but that's kind of further inland. We are on shale here. So I started looking up sand as a heat-holder, due to the fact that I didn't really want to buy some rocks, as they are expensive, used mostly for decoration in front yards, or fire pits. I was pleased to find that sand does indeed hold heat extremely well. The sand we have is mixed with organic debris, but it can be cleaned - I have done that a lot in making some homemade fertilizer. Pure white sand is the result. We have power outages here once in awhile, but being in Florida, heat is not too much of concern, as our temps stay pretty moderate most of the year. We don't see freezing too often. Daytimes can hang around 50 or so in the winter. But putting a bucket of hot sand from a fire pit in the yard under my chair could be a warm welcome if the electric goes out in the wintertime.

author: @shapnotts2517
text:
So, I'm wondering about such a system for a future self-build. I currently have approx 2KW vacuum tube solar arrays feeding a massive (in house terms anyway) 400l water cylinder. This works in conjunction with a gas boiler and wood boiler. For a 3000 ftsq house the gas boiler is a tiny 15kw output and generally keeps the house warm in the winter (with back up from the wood boiler if it gets a bit colder). The solar heating is always making some contribution.
The whole system remains a bit Heath Robinson to be fair and does require fairly constant weather-checking and manual fire-lighting at night to top up the water cylinder (heat store) after a dull few days. Generally though, I don't need the gas boiler at all from April to late September in the UK.
The problem in the summer is over-heating and water in the solar primary pipework boiling away (sometimes alarmingly).
Now, a sand / rock, soil battery as a heat sump under the house would appear to be a great idea and could alleviate the water boiling problem. I'm just wondering what size of hole under the floor would be required to heat a typical 3-4 bed house over the winter. I'm wondering if a water-based under-floor sand store piped system fed by solar arrays could work.


Just stumbled upon your channel by the way. Love it!

text: I remember reading an article long ago about a cinder block container filled with sand and a fire box was built into it as well as copper water coils throughout the sand. replies.author: @alfredharrison597
replies.text: Put a copper coil tubing surrounded by the wet sand that you heated up (inside the heater) and the copper tubing going to a radiator inlet and outlet. Much like a pass through system. Fill the radiator up with antifreeze and make sure there are no leaks. What this will do is circulate the hot antifreeze inside the tank with the wet heated up sand outside and that circulation will keep the radiator heater heated all night long. I watched a guy do this in alaska where the temp outside was below zero and it kept him warm inside his little shack he build comfortable all night long. He was very far from freezing to death. He had his heating unit sitting outside in a campfire but the same premise applies here also. Can also use an H2 burner (if youre living off grid and doing the H2 generation thing) to keep the heater warm, much like a gas-powered hot water heater.

Combustibles are the option. Producing H2 is not efficient on a small scale. Why waste energy. I’d say for off grid, build a solid solar array and us battery backup and build a solar reflector to heat water during the day and pump that into your sand through copper pipes to heat the interior and have a huge sand container with plenty of piping.

author: @markusgarvey
text:
You just gave me a great idea. Thanks again Professor.

 I have big dirt and rock berms on my land, mostly clay. (I have a lot of very dense basalt rock on my land. The clay relatively fresh volcanic ash, with not much else) The rock is so dense, you have a hard time breaking it w/ hammer and chisel. I am a Mason as well, so I know what hard is.

Rocket stove, still heating water heating,  w/ pipes coiled in the berms. I can add sand where I excavate for the water line. I will also coil the lines under my floor and run a small circulating pump when there is no thermosyphon. I live off grid, and I have to run generators during our really cold winter, so I am gonna capture all the exhaust heat  with a heat exchanger, and put it in the system.

Win-win. I always think about all the energy wasted from exhaust heat. Now to figure out how to use the heat to cool as well. I will send you a video. 

I wonder if the rock has has a good storage capacity, but I don't have the equipment to find out yet.

replies.text: This reminds me of my clothes dryer. I had the brilliant idea to use foam insulation all around it and then a put a small duct fan in a 10in duct and pumped the hot air out of my attic through the dryer. The dryer sucks in ambient air and heats it and pumps it outside normally. So when I did this to my dryer, I reduced the temperature in the attic which reduces the houses cooling cost and also it reduced the heating element required in the dryer, they use a sensor to turn on and off the element. It worked amazingly well at reducing my power. I just through I would share because it is an interesting way to save power and it really does work!!

The problem is temperature.
If you have a home with a surplus heat during day, it will be at 25-30c max. At night you need the heat and the available heat has to be above 20-25c.

replies.author: @suzannehartmann946
replies.text: The Romans ran a pipe beneath the floor of their homes.  The fire was on one side the pipe ran slightly uphill beneath the floor warming the floor and the home. Smoke escaped the pipe the other side. Add sand or water on either side of the pipe to retain and radiate the heat and you have a more efficient system.  Run the pipe back and forth (left and right) as you see in old  indoor radiators (hot water pipes with radiators in individual rooms for warmth) but instead of running hot water up and down you run the hot air back and forth through sand (water will eventually rust the pipes) and you would probably have a great system. It would also prevent smoke entering the home though cracks in the pipes. Having a furnace beneath the home was common in places with basements in eastern USA where feasible. Florida not so much.

Although I knew someone who used a similar tactic by heating rocks during the day, and then moving them inside at night in the mountains of KY.  So unless you are in the dark, u can make heat on cold days passively.  Sometimes simple stuff like putting a radiant barrier in your attic and/or making sure there is a jog in your exhaust pipe can keep what heat you do get inside.  But I live in Fl, I have a fireplace which is surrounded by rock ... so that one day a year I do use it... it stays hot/warm for 12+ hours or so after burning.  yes, most of the heat escapes.  but the flue column surrounding the pipe also is a contained air gap, I could steal heat with a small fan to reduce cold spots in the room ... but like I said, I don't need it.  Personally I'd try a closed loop of mineral oil, although i know nothing about how to passively pump it into a radiator. glhf.

author: @VinoVeritas_
text: Have you ever looked into Sodium Acetate Trihydrate as a medium for energy storage? Phase change occurs at 58°C and the latent heat of fusion is 270kJ per kg. Density is around 1.5kg per litre. Also, you need to consider all the CO² and Methane in there that can be acquired through sequestration and locked away.

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: I've mentioned this previously in another comment on the other sand battery video, but the main challenges facing SAT as a thermal energy storage medium is phase separation when in its supercooled state. An addition of CMC seems to do the trick with that though. The other issue is the need to improve thermal conductivity. One research paper added graphite to improve the situation, but I haven't seen any mention of graphene yet. The last major issue is with the initiation of nucleation when the SAT is in its supercooled state. It's very simple to do it physically by adding SAT crystals to the supercooled liquid to kick-start the crystallisation process and release that previously mentioned 270kJ per kg of latent heat of fusion. That 58°C temperature is much easier to work with as well.

replies.author: @petercoutu4726
replies.text:
I have been trying to work a way to build this kind of SAT energy storage/ emergency heating system. My thought is that it would be contained within a large vacuum flask/dwar that has a loop of copper around the inner flask and a central column that contains the electric heating element and another immersed heat exchange coil which is used in tandem with the interior wall of the vacuum flask, to either extract the heat energy once nucleation is triggered or to recharge the system with domestic hydronic heating or through solar thermal collection. This is in addition to the electric element that has potential for being run directly from the grid using off peak prices, being rechargeable during power outages with a portable generator that isn't suitable for all electric heating systems. I also see it as an option for use as a dump load for most renewable energy sources such as hydro, and wind. 

Not being a trained chemical expert, I am curious about what other substances may have a similar effect to sodium acetate, in addition to what additives can be useful.
